title: Energy security: The foundation of job creation
gist: Global gas flaring hit 167 bcm in 2025 (Africa's entire annual consumption) and rose for a third straight year; capturing that gas is a ~2-year-payback investment that boosts energy security, fiscal balances, and jobs, with Kazakhstan and Argentina as proof cases.

insights.claim: In 2025 the world flared 167 billion cubic meters of gas at oilfields, equivalent to Africa's entire annual gas consumption, with flaring rising for the third consecutive year.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Persistent flaring growth signals structural underinvestment in gas-capture infrastructure and a recurring supply of near-free feedstock for anyone building capture capacity.
insights.quote: in 2025 the world flared 167 billion cubic meters (bcm) of gas, the equivalent of Africa's entire annual gas consumption

insights.claim: The world burns roughly $54 billion worth of gas annually through flaring, an economic loss distinct from and additional to the climate cost.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Quantifies the addressable prize; frames flaring capture as a cost-recovery play rather than a pure ESG expense.

themes.theme: Energy security and the investment case

insights.claim: Many of the world's largest flaring countries simultaneously import gas at high cost while burning their own associated gas, leaving power sectors needlessly exposed to supply shocks.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Identifies a clear policy-arbitrage: import substitution via domestic gas capture is a fiscal win for gas-importing oil producers, a catalyst to watch for regulatory change.
insights.quote: This leaves their power sectors needlessly exposed to supply shocks and their citizens needlessly paying for energy they already have.
insights.locator: And yet at the same time

insights.claim: Eliminating routine gas flaring globally would require about $70-100 billion of investment, with a payback period of roughly two years against the $54 billion of gas burned annually, followed by durable revenues, import savings, and tax receipts.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: A two-year payback with long revenue tails makes gas-capture infrastructure an attractive target for development finance and private capital in oil-producing frontier markets.
insights.quote: Eliminating routine flaring globally would require about $70 billion to $100 billion.

insights.claim: For gas-importing oil producers like Egypt, India, and Iraq, every cubic meter redirected from the flare stack to market is a cubic meter of costly imported gas avoided, making flaring capture a fiscal policy as much as an energy one.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Reframes flaring reduction around budget relief and subsidy savings, which is likelier to move finance ministries than emissions arguments alone.
insights.locator: For countries that import significant volumes

insights.claim: Reliable electricity is a prerequisite for private investment, and uncertainty over power availability or cost pushes job-creating projects to other locations.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Power reliability is a leading indicator of FDI attractiveness; grid stability metrics are a proxy for a country's investment pipeline.
insights.quote: When investors face uncertainty over power availability or costs, projects become less attractive and investments that could have created jobs go somewhere else instead.

insights.claim: Kazakhstan reduced its gas flaring by 87% over the last decade through sustained regulatory enforcement and infrastructure investment.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that firm regulation plus capex can nearly eliminate flaring, a template for other producers and a signal that enforcement, not technology, is the binding constraint.
insights.quote: Kazakhstan, for example, reduced its flaring by 87% over the last decade through sustained regulatory enforcement and infrastructure investment.

insights.claim: Argentina is cutting flaring in its fast-growing Vaca Muerta basin by expanding pipelines and processing capacity, showing production growth and flaring reduction can occur simultaneously.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Midstream buildout (pipelines, processing) in Vaca Muerta is where the flaring-reduction value accrues; watch Argentine infrastructure capex.

insights.claim: Capturing associated gas for domestic power creates direct high-value jobs (engineers, technicians, construction) plus indirect gains as small businesses and manufacturers can run reliably and attract prior-unavailable investment.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Positions flaring capture as an employment-multiplier story, useful for aligning political will and development-bank mandates behind the capex.
insights.locator: Capturing flared gas is an economic multiplier

insights.claim: Two billion people still use polluting fuels and technologies for cooking, and associated gas can be processed into LPG, one of the most accessible clean cooking fuels.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: A two-billion-person clean-cooking gap is a large latent LPG demand pool that associated-gas capture could supply.
insights.quote: two billion people use polluting fuels and technologies for cooking, putting their health and well-being at risk
insights.locator: Associated gas can also be processed into LPG

insights.claim: Redirecting associated gas to LPG reduces the time women spend collecting biomass fuel, freeing hours for paid work, education, and entrepreneurship.
insights.speaker: Zubin Bamji
insights.so_what: Clean-cooking transitions carry a female labor-participation dividend, a second-order effect relevant to gender-lens and impact investors.
